subject: [PATCH v2 0/4] mm: improve large folio readahead and alignment for exec memory

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that mapping_max_folio_size() returns a conservative value on some file systems, such as ext4, which can lead to missed optimization opportunities for large folio alignment and coalescing PTEs. He suggested allowing a reasonably sized PMD (up to 32M) and aligning the binary to this size when possible, leaving the decision to collapse or not to THP under always.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Usama,

On Fri, Mar 20, 2026 at 10:04\u202fPM Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> diff --git a/fs/binfmt_elf.c b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
> index 8e89cc5b28200..042af81766fcd 100644
> --- a/fs/binfmt_elf.c
> +++ b/fs/binfmt_elf.c
> @@ -49,6 +49,7 @@
>  #include <uapi/linux/rseq.h>
>  #include <asm/param.h>
>  #include <asm/page.h>
> +#include <linux/pagemap.h>
>
>  #ifndef ELF_COMPAT
>  #define ELF_COMPAT 0
> @@ -488,19 +489,51 @@ static int elf_read(struct file *file, void *buf, size_t len, loff_t pos)
>         return 0;
>  }
>
> -static unsigned long maximum_alignment(struct elf_phdr *cmds, int nr)
> +static unsigned long maximum_alignment(struct elf_phdr *cmds, int nr,
> +                                      struct file *filp)
>  {
>         unsigned long alignment = 0;
> +       unsigned long max_folio_size = PAGE_SIZE;
>         int i;
>
> +       if (filp && filp->f_mapping)
> +               max_folio_size = mapping_max_folio_size(filp->f_mapping);

>From experiments (with 16K base pages), mapping_max_folio_size() appears to
depend on the filesystem. It returns 8M on ext4, while on btrfs it always
falls back to PAGE_SIZE (it seems CONFIG_BTRFS_EXPERIMENTAL=y may change this).
This looks overly conservative and ends up missing practical optimization
opportunities.

> +
>         for (i = 0; i < nr; i++) {
>                 if (cmds[i].p_type == PT_LOAD) {
>                         unsigned long p_align = cmds[i].p_align;
> +                       unsigned long size;
>
>                         /* skip non-power of two alignments as invalid */
>                         if (!is_power_of_2(p_align))
>                                 continue;
>                         alignment = max(alignment, p_align);
> +
> +                       /*
> +                        * Try to align the binary to the largest folio
> +                        * size that the page cache supports, so the
> +                        * hardware can coalesce PTEs (e.g. arm64
> +                        * contpte) or use PMD mappings for large folios.
> +                        *
> +                        * Use the largest power-of-2 that fits within
> +                        * the segment size, capped by what the page
> +                        * cache will allocate. Only align when the
> +                        * segment's virtual address and file offset are
> +                        * already aligned to the folio size, as
> +                        * misalignment would prevent coalescing anyway.
> +                        *
> +                        * The segment size check avoids reducing ASLR
> +                        * entropy for small binaries that cannot
> +                        * benefit.
> +                        */
> +                       if (!cmds[i].p_filesz)
> +                               continue;
> +                       size = rounddown_pow_of_two(cmds[i].p_filesz);
> +                       size = min(size, max_folio_size);
> +                       if (size > PAGE_SIZE &&
> +                           IS_ALIGNED(cmds[i].p_vaddr, size) &&
> +                           IS_ALIGNED(cmds[i].p_offset, size))
> +                               alignment = max(alignment, size);

In my patch [1], by aligning eligible segments to PMD_SIZE, THP can quickly
collapse them into large mappings with minimal warmup. That doesn\u2019t happen
with the current behavior. I think allowing a reasonably sized PMD (say <= 32M)
is worth considering. All we really need here is to ensure virtual address
alignment. The rest can be left to THP under always, which can decide whether
to collapse or not based on memory pressure and other factors.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-fsdevel/20260313005211.882831-1-r@hev.cc

>                 }
>         }
>
> @@ -1104,7 +1137,8 @@ static int load_elf_binary(struct linux_binprm *bprm)
>                         }
>
>                         /* Calculate any requested alignment. */
> -                       alignment = maximum_alignment(elf_phdata, elf_ex->e_phnum);
> +                       alignment = maximum_alignment(elf_phdata, elf_ex->e_phnum,
> +                                                     bprm->file);
>
>                         /**
>                          * DOC: PIE handling
> --
> 2.52.0
>

Thanks,
Rui

---

> mapping_max_folio_size() reflects what the page cache will actually
> allocate for a given filesystem, since readahead caps folio allocation
> at mapping_max_folio_order() (in page_cache_ra_order()). If btrfs
> reports PAGE_SIZE, readahead won't allocate large folios for it, so
> there are no large folios to coalesce PTEs for, aligning the binary
> beyond that would only reduce ASLR entropy for no benefit.
> 
> I don't think we should over-align binaries on filesystems that can't
> take advantage of it.

Ah, it looks like this might be overlooking another path that can create
huge page mappings for read-only code segments: even when the filesystem
(e.g. btrfs without experimental) didn't support large folios,
READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS still allowed read-only file-backed code segments
to be collapsed into huge page mappings via khugepaged.

As Wilcox pointed out, it may take quite some time for many filesystems
to gain full large folio support? So what I'm trying to clarify is that
using mapping_max_folio_size() on this path is not favorable for
khugepaged-based optimizations.

Thanks,
Rui

2026-03-21.patch_summary: This patch series improves large folio readahead and alignment for executable memory by introducing a generic, architecture-independent solution to replace the arch-specific exec_folio_order() function. The patches address two main issues: the mmap_miss heuristic that disables readahead after 100 page faults, and the suboptimal static order returned by exec_folio_order() for non-4K page sizes. The new approach uses preferred_exec_order() to sync mmap_readahead with zone high watermarks, and extends max alignment to mapping_max_folio_size().

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the mmap_miss heuristic in do_sync_mmap_readahead() silently disables exec readahead after 100 page faults, agreed to restructure in v2 to exclude VM_EXEC VMAs from this logic.

Author questioned the necessity of an explicit zone_watermark_ok() loop in page_cache_ra_order(), suggesting that readahead_gfp_mask() already handles memory pressure by setting __GFP_NORETRY, and proposed a simpler approach to set ra->order.
reviews.raw_body:
Hi Jan,

Thanks for the feedback and sorry for the late reply! I was travelling
during the week.

---

So I have been thinking about this. readahead_gfp_mask() already sets
__GFP_NORETRY, so we wont try aggressive reclaim/compaction to satisfy
the allocation. page_cache_ra_order() falls through to the fallback path
faulting in order 0 page when allocation is not satsified.

So the allocator already naturally steps down under memory pressure,
the explicit zone_watermark_ok() loop might be redundant?

What are your thoughts on just setting
ra->order = min(HPAGE_PMD_ORDER, ilog2(SZ_2M >> PAGE_SHIFT))?
We can do the higher orlder allocation with gfp &= ~__GFP_RECLAIM
for the VM_EXEC case.

reviews.author: Jan Kara
reviews.summary:
Jan Kara expressed concern that the used order in the patch may be too large for systems with limited memory, suggesting that smaller folios would be beneficial and allow for finer-grained tracking of folio activity.

Reviewer Jan Kara expressed general agreement and approval for the patch, stating that it is simple and makes sense to him.
reviews.raw_body:
Probably yes. I still think we'll have to somehow better tweak the used
order based on expected size of the page cache (2M folios seem unreasonably
large for a machine that has e.g. 1G of memory in total, even if it has
enough free memory at this point in time - we'll benefit from smaller
folios and thus finer grained folio activity tracking for such cases). But
that's not for this patch set.

---

Yes, it's simple and it makes sense to me so if others are fine with it...

								Honza
-- 
Jan Kara <jack@suse.com>
SUSE Labs, CR

2026-03-26.patch_summary: This patch series improves large folio readahead and alignment for executable memory by introducing a generic, architecture-independent solution to replace the arch-specific exec_folio_order() function. The patches address two main issues: the mmap_miss heuristic that disables readahead after 100 page faults, and the suboptimal static order returned by exec_folio_order() for non-4K page sizes. The new approach uses preferred_exec_order() to sync mmap_readahead with zone high watermarks, and extends max alignment to mapping_max_folio_size().

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary:
The author acknowledged that the `mmap_miss` logic for VM_EXEC is not necessary in this context, agreeing to remove it.

Author acknowledged a need to address a concern about disabling readahead after 100 page faults in do_sync_mmap_readahead(), agreed to restructure in v2
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah its unnecessary here. Its used in other places and this is never
checked, so we can remove it.

---

ack, will try and address this accordingly.


Thanks for the reviews!!

reviews.author: Usama Arif (author)
reviews.summary: The author addressed a concern about the effectiveness of aligning binaries beyond what the page cache will allocate, explaining that doing so would only reduce ASLR entropy for no benefit on filesystems like btrfs that report PAGE_SIZE.
reviews.raw_body:
mapping_max_folio_size() reflects what the page cache will actually
allocate for a given filesystem, since readahead caps folio allocation
at mapping_max_folio_order() (in page_cache_ra_order()). If btrfs
reports PAGE_SIZE, readahead won't allocate large folios for it, so
there are no large folios to coalesce PTEs for, aligning the binary
beyond that would only reduce ASLR entropy for no benefit.

I don't think we should over-align binaries on filesystems that can't
take advantage of it.

2026-03-27.patch_summary: This patch series improves large folio readahead and alignment for executable memory by introducing a generic, architecture-independent solution to replace the arch-specific exec_folio_order() function. The patches address two main issues: the mmap_miss heuristic that disables readahead after 100 page faults, and the suboptimal static order returned by exec_folio_order() for non-4K page sizes. The new approach uses preferred_exec_order() to sync mmap_readahead with zone high watermarks, and extends max alignment to mapping_max_folio_size().

reviews.author: WANG Rui
reviews.summary: Reviewer WANG Rui noted that the patch may overlook another path creating huge page mappings for read-only code segments, specifically through khugepaged even when filesystems don't support large folios, and suggested using a different approach to optimize khugepaged-based mappings.
reviews.raw_body:
Ah, it looks like this might be overlooking another path that can create
huge page mappings for read-only code segments: even when the filesystem
(e.g. btrfs without experimental) didn't support large folios,
READ_ONLY_THP_FOR_FS still allowed read-only file-backed code segments
to be collapsed into huge page mappings via khugepaged.

As Wilcox pointed out, it may take quite some time for many filesystems
to gain full large folio support? So what I'm trying to clarify is that
using mapping_max_folio_size() on this path is not favorable for
khugepaged-based optimizations.

Thanks,
Rui
